aliases: Chicken in Black Bean Sauce with Rice
Black bean chicken with steamed rice

english: Chicken in Black Bean Sauce with Rice
Black bean chicken with steamed rice

food_subgroup: Braised or stewed chicken with rice, served with fermented black bean sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for black bean sauce chicken rice. FOODON_00002572 (chicken dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (chicken meat dish) used as closest parent; no specific code exists for black bean sauce chicken rice in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific prepared dish.

allergens: gluten (from soy sauce and oyster sauce)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of black bean sauce chicken rice, comprising approximately 200 g of steamed white rice and 180 g of braised chicken pieces in black bean sauce, totalling approximately 380 g.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken with black bean sauce and steamed white rice — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central: Chicken thigh meat cooked (FDC ID 331960), Fermented black bean sauce (FDC ID 2103689), and Cooked white rice (FDC ID 169756); proportions based on standard Filipino-style preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for black bean sauce chicken rice exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: approximately 47% steamed white rice, 43% cooked chicken thigh, and 10% black bean sauce and seasonings by weight. Energy and macros are weighted composites. Sodium is elevated due to fermented black bean sauce and soy sauce content.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (boiled) GI 64–72; mixed chicken-and-rice dish estimated at GI 56 based on protein and fat attenuation of rice GI
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for black bean sauce chicken rice. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on: (1) boiled white rice GI of approximately 64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database; (2) attenuation effect of co-ingested protein (~9.8 g/100g) and fat (~4.2 g/100g) from chicken and sauce, which is well-documented to reduce the glycemic response of rice-based mixed meals (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2021; Wolever et al., Am J Clin Nutr 1994). Glycemic load per 380 g serving calculated as: (56 × 66.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 37.2, rounded to 37.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fermented black beans and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: A single 380 g serving provides approximately 1,596 mg sodium, representing over 69% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce portions or limit frequency of consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by white rice component, partially attenuated by protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated GI of 56 and glycemic load of 37 per serving indicate a moderate glycemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion to approximately 100–150 g and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables to lower the overall glycemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from chicken skin and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~420 mg/100g) and moderate saturated fat (~1.1 g/100g) are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose skinless chicken, request lighter sauce, and limit portion size to manage sodium and saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Bean Sauce Chicken Rice is a Filipino-influenced dish popular in Singapore's casual dining and café scene, consisting of tender braised or stir-fried chicken pieces coated in a savoury, umami-rich fermented black bean (douchi) sauce, served over a bed of steamed white rice. The fermented black beans — a staple of Chinese culinary tradition widely adopted in Filipino cooking — impart a deeply savoury, slightly salty, and earthy flavour profile, complemented by garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and oyster sauce. The chicken is typically bone-in thigh or drumstick pieces, yielding a moist and tender texture after braising. The dish is a complete one-plate meal providing moderate energy (~148 kcal/100g), good-quality animal protein (~9.8 g/100g), and carbohydrate from white rice (~17.5 g/100g). Sodium content is notably high (~420 mg/100g) due to the fermented black bean sauce and soy-based seasonings, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 56 (medium), as the protein and fat from the chicken partially attenuate the glycemic response of the white rice. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, portion control of the rice component is advisable. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with shellfish or soy allergies. It is commonly found in Filipino cafés, kopitiam-style eateries, and casual rice-plate restaurants across Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for black bean sauce chicken rice was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked chicken thigh, steamed white rice, and fermented black bean sauce in proportions consistent with standard Filipino-style preparation. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database with documented protein-fat attenuation. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates from USDA FoodData Central component entries; no single approved-source entry for this exact dish was identified. Values should be updated if an exact HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry becomes available. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this estimate. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this estimate. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this estimate. 5. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed negligible/trace per USDA component data for chicken and rice — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 6. GI is an analogous estimate (56, medium) based on white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database attenuated by co-ingested protein and fat; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in approved sources. 7. Halal status is marked false as the dish may contain oyster sauce and the halal certification status of the specific restaurant (Lucky Cafe - North Canal Road) is not confirmed. 8. The cuisine is listed as Filipino per the input source (field survey data); the dish reflects Chinese-Filipino culinary fusion with fermented black bean sauce (douchi) as the defining ingredient.
